title: Lawrence Kasdan
content:
{{Top|rwp}} {{Otheruses|Lawrence}} {{Multiple_issues|expand|image}} {{Person |image=[[File:LawrenceKasdan.jpg]] |name=Lawrence Kasdan |birth=[[January 14]], [[1940s#1949|1949]]
[[Wikipedia:Miami Beach|Miami Beach]], [[Wikipedia:Florida|Florida]], [[Wikipedia:United States of America|United States]]{{WebCite|url=https://www.tcm.com/tcmdb/person/98881%7C0/Lawrence-Kasdan#overview|text=Lawrence Kasdan - Turner Classic Movies|work=TCM|archivedate=20110928091325}} |death= |listedpronouns= |nationality=[[Wikipedia:Americans|American]] |occupation= |companies=Producer, director, screenwriter |website= }} '''Lawrence Kasdan''' (born [[January 14]], [[1940s#1949|1949]]) is an American movie producer, director and screenwriter. He co-wrote [[Star Wars: Episode V The Empire Strikes Back|''The Empire Strikes Back'']], [[Star Wars: Episode VI Return of the Jedi|''Return of the Jedi'']], [[Star Wars: Episode VII The Force Awakens|''The Force Awakens'']], and ''[[Solo: A Star Wars Story]]''. He also wrote [[Lucasfilm Ltd.|Lucasfilm's]] [[Wikipedia:Raiders of the Lost Ark|''Raiders of the Lost Ark'']]. Outside of his work for Lucasfilm, Kasdan is known for both writing and directing his films, which have ranged from Westerns to romantic comedies to thought-provoking dramas. He has received four Academy Award nominations, for screenplays to ''The Big Chill'', ''Grand Canyon'', and ''The Accidental Tourist'', for which he also earned a directing nod. ==Biography== ===Early life=== Lawrence Edward Kasdan was born on [[January 14]], [[1940s#1949|1949]] in [[Wikipedia:Miami Beach|Miami Beach]], [[Wikipedia:Florida|Florida]]. ===''The Empire Strikes Back''=== In [[1978]], [[George Lucas]] commissioned Lawrence Kasdan, who was in midst of revising his own [[W:c:indianajones:Raiders of the Lost Ark|''Raiders of the Lost Ark'']] script for both him and [[Steven Spielberg]], to complete the screenplay for [[Star Wars: Episode V The Empire Strikes Back|''The Empire Strikes Back'']] after the death of [[Leigh Brackett]], whose work hadn't satisfied Lucas, leading him to write the next draft himself, which included much of the main plot elements and characters seen in the finished film, but lacked good dialogue. Lucas had yet to even read Kasdan's first draft of ''Raiders'', but asked him to rewrite Brackett's script when Kasdan delivered it with Lucas simply saying he would withdraw the offer the next day if he disliked ''Raiders''.Wright, Jim (November 7, 1981). "From 'Raiders' to 'Body Heat': Kasdan has winning combination". ''The Miami Herald''. Lucas ultimately hired Kasdan to co-write all subsequent ''Empire'' drafts out of desperation, given that he didn't have anyone else available for the job.''Skywalking: The Life and Films of George Lucas'' Despite his admiration for Brackett,{{WebCite|url=https://www.vanityfair.com/hollywood/2015/05/lawrence-kasdan-star-wars-force-awakens|text=Lawrence Kasdan Says There's a Future for Lando Calrissian in Star Wars|date=2015-05-18|work=[[Wikipedia:Vanity Fair|''Vanity Fair'']]|archivedate=20181221015756}} Kasdan opined that Brackett's script was sort of old-fashioned, feeling that the story's spirit was different despite the characters being the same.''Mythmaker: The Life and Work of George Lucas'' In the end, Lucas opted to read Kasdan's script for ''Raiders'' first and liked it, hiring him almost immediately, as the crew were in pre-production and lacked an script, though in contrast to Kasdan's assignment to write ''Raiders'', Lucas had conceived the whole story for ''The Empire Strikes Back'' by then and just needed Kasdan to get the script done and approved by [[Irvin Kershner]], the film's director. Fortunately for Lucas, Kasdan was a fan of genre movies and the films by [[Wikipedia:Akira Kurosawa|Akira Kurosawa]] and [[Wikipedia:Howard Hawks|Howard Hawks]] while able to write playful romance, broad humor, personal intimacies and infusing characters with a thoroughly emotional subtext.{{InsiderCite|49|Lawrence Kasdan: Screenwriter}} Once hired, Kasdan was informed by Lucas that one of the plot points in the script was that [[Anakin Skywalker|Darth Vader]] would be revealed as [[Luke Skywalker]]'s [[Parent|father]], which was a total surprise for Kasdan. Unaware of what elements from Brackett's draft were included in Lucas', Kasdan felt that Lucas' draft was a rough first draft somewhere between a film outline and a complete first draft, like if it were a "skeleton for a movie" which urged "flesh and muscle."''Cinefantastique'' magazine, February 1997 Kasdan decided to make the story of ''The Empire Strikes Back'' darker than that of the first ''[[Star Wars: Episode IV A New Hope|Star Wars]]'', a decision Lucas agreed with as he had envisioned a trajectory for the [[Original trilogy|first three ''Star Wars'' films]] and ''The Empire Strikes Back'' was going to be the second act, where traditionally the protagonists of a story face a difficult situation,''Back Story 4: Interviews with Screenwriters of the 1970s and 1980s'' with Kershner likening the script's tone to that of the gloomy fairy tales of the [[Wikipedia:Brothers Grimm|Brothers Grimm]]. In November [[1978]], with filming slated to start in five months, Kasdan reunited with Lucas, Kershner and producer [[Gary Kurtz]] for a story conference. During the next two weeks, Lucas explained to Kasdan the purpose of some scenes, what he wanted to achieve dramatically and how he could improve them, requesting the script to be no longer than 105 pages. Kasdan and Lucas agreed with Kershner and Kurtz that ''Empire'' had to grapple on the philosophical issues raised by the original film but in a quick an unraveled way, leaving some questions unanswered for the audience's imagination. Kasdan thought that Lucas was sometimes committing a mistake by hurrying up an scene to get to the next one and skipping its emotional content, a sentiment shared by Kurtz despite Lucas' belief that if the action was enough, the audience would not notice it. Kasdan felt that whereas he was interested in exploring the relationships between characters and their eccentricities, Lucas cared more on moving the plot forward.''Baltimore Sun'', May 2002 While reading Lucas' drafts, Kasdan read some sections that were terrible on his opinion. He thought that the movie could hold more complexity and character instead of being simpler like Lucas thought. After taking notes from the conference, Kasdan went back to [[Wikipedia:Los Angeles|Los Angeles]] and returned with a twenty-five pages draft for ''Empire''. As the script was developed, Kasdan added some [[Wikipedia:Zen|Zen Buddhism]] overtones to the [[Yoda]] character that became pronounced once Kershner, a Buddhist like Kasdan, reshaped the material along with him. Yoda's characteristic speech patterns were conceived by Kasdan and Lucas, and were later developed by [[Frank Oz]].{{YouTube|video=0BN-l4r2eIA|text=Frank Oz: In confidence - Full Interview (2013)|channel=channel/UC8JDnDIydpU-nbh2S8is7rQ|name=VHSfx|archivedate=20170114184223}} A character created by Kasdan was that of [[Admiral]] [[Firmus Piett|Piett]], as Kasdan knew that there was fun in giving some grist to background characters to help the viewers identify them and and remind the audience that some [[Galactic Empire|Imperials]] were people doing their jobs.{{WebCite|url=https://www.theatlantic.com/entertainment/archive/2015/12/the-ballad-of-admiral-piett/420570|text=The Ballad of Admiral Piett|author=Sims, David|date=2015-12-15|work=[[Wikipedia:The Atlantic|''The Atlantic'']]|archivedate=20151215204245}} The shooting script for ''Empire'' was eventually finished by Kasdan, with some minor changes to dialogues added by Kershner and actors throughout the filming, which were mostly approved by Lucas.''[[The Making of The Empire Strikes Back]]'' Kasdan initially objected to the on-set improvisations, but eventually warmed up to [[Harrison Ford]]'s improvisation of the line "I know" despite that he didn't write it.{{WebCite|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2018/05/30/movies/star-wars-lawrence-kasdan.html|text=He's Tried to Leave 'Star Wars' Before. Will This Be It?|author=Itzkoff, Dave|date=2018-05-30|work=[[Wikipedia:The New York Times|''The New York Times'']]|archivedate=20180530223747}} ''The Empire Strikes Back'' was released on [[May 21]], [[1980]]. One of the film's aspects critics mostly praised was incidentally the character development. It also marked the first time Kasdan's name appeared in a film's credits. In retrospective, Kasdan stated [[1981|a year later]] that he felt that his major contribution to the [[Star Wars saga|''Star Wars'' saga]] was indeed the character development of the main characters.Stark, Susan (September 20, 1981). "Hollywood's new hotshot". ''The Detroit News''. He also went to praise Kersher's direction, style and his ability to convince Lucas to do some things differently as planned. ===''Return of the Jedi''=== Following the release of ''The Empire Strikes Back'', Kasdan began his directing career and expressed no interest in writing another ''[[Star Wars]]'' movie. However, George Lucas supported him as an uncredited producer for [[Wikipedia:Body Heat|''Body Heat'']], his [[Wikipedia:Directorial debut|directorial debut]], so Kasdan felt that he owed him a favor when Lucas approached him to write [[Star Wars: Episode VI Return of the Jedi|''Return of the Jedi'']], the follow-up to ''The Empire Strikes Back''.Byron, Stuart (August 25, 1981). "Rules of the Game". ''Village Voice''. When approached, Kasdan was preparing himself right then to direct [[Wikipedia:The Big Chill (film)|''The Big Chill'']] and no longer was writing movies for other filmmakers, but from his point of view, Lucas had given him a career and needed his help once more. Before rehiring Kasdan, Lucas had also hired [[Richard Marquand]], who would serve as the director for ''Return of the Jedi'' as Kershner had read an scaled-down version of the script for ''Jedi'' but didn't believe in it, hence part of his disinterest in returning.Krebs, Josef (October 2004). "The Empire Strikes Back Director: Irvin Kershner". ''Sound and Vision''. On [[June 12]], [[1981]], upon the completition of Lucas' revised rough draft , Kasdan started to write the shooting script for ''Return of the Jedi'' based on a story by Lucas. With the script ready in the form of a rough draft, Kasdan attended some story meetings with Lucas, Marquand and producer [[Howard G. Kazanjian]] between [[July 13]] and [[July 17]].''[[Star Wars: The Annotated Screenplays]]'' During these meetings, the draft written by Lucas was changed considerably, dropping, altering and expanding some ideas and suggesting others.Patterson, Richard (June 1983). "Producing and Directing Return of the Jedi". ''American cinematographer''. In one of the meetings, Kasdan proposed along with Marquand the idea of opening the film featuring the [[Rescue of Han Solo|rescue]] of [[Han Solo]], a sequence which was more simpler in Lucas' rought draft, with Kasdan suggesting killing off [[Jabba Desilijic Tiure|Jabba]] the [[Hutt]] during the sequence.''[[The Making of Return of the Jedi]]'' Lucas additionally suggested Kasdan to write a great scene featuring [[Obi-Wan Kenobi]] as a [[Force spirit|Force ghost]] with powerful [[Wikipedia:William Shakespeare|Shakespearian]] dialogue for Sir [[Alec Guinness]]. In an effort to one-up the first film, Lucas initially wrote two [[Death Star]]s and [[Had Abbadon/Legends|Had Abbadon]], the Imperial [[homeworld]], but Kasdan suggested to combine all three of them into [[DS-2 Death Star II Mobile Battle Station|a single threat]], leading Lucas to conclude that it wasn't a good idea to show Vader and the Emperor in an area unrelated to the story and that it wasn't financially possible to design the Imperial [[planet]]. With respect to the Emperor, Lucas desired to establish him as the true source of evil in the saga, to which Kasdan noted that the relationship between Vader and the Emperor was that the latter was much more powerful and intimidating, posessing all the power while Vader posessed dignity. Lucas also shared with Kasdan, Marquand and Kazanjian some of his early ideas for the eventual ''Star Wars'' [[prequel trilogy]], including how the [[Darth Sidious|Emperor]] [[Proclamation of the New Order|took over]] the [[Galactic Senate]] and [[Duel in Palpatine's office|turned]] Anakin Skywalker to the [[dark side of the Force]] or how all people could use [[the Force]] like the [[Jedi]], comparing it to [[Wikipedia:Yoga|yoga]] or [[Wikipedia:Karate|karate]]. When the meetings ended, Kasdan was left with an immense transcript full of confirmed decisions and rejected ideas about how the script should be formed. While writing the screenplay, Kasdan felt that the film lacked any real gravity and proposed to kill off one of the main characters during the beginning of the film's final act to make the audience doubt on whether the heroes would survive unscathed or if there would be even a happy ending.''[[Empire of Dreams: The Story of the Star Wars Trilogy]]'' Kasdan suggested to maybe kill Luke and allow [[Leia Skywalker Organa Solo|Leia Organa]] to take over his role, but Lucas disagreed because it reminded him how upset he ended up as a child if his hero in a movie was killed. In turn, Harrison Ford suggested to kill off [[Han Solo]] by having him sacrificing himself for Luke and Leia because he felt that his character wasn't part of the bigger story and lacked family, responsibilities, and future. Kasdan supported Ford, having been in favor of killing Solo since ''The Empire Strikes Back'' but feeling that ''Return of the Jedi'' was the right movie to do it, as they were ending the trilogy, but Lucas also refused.{{WebCite|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2018/05/30/movies/star-wars-lawrence-kasdan.html|text=He's Tried to Leave 'Star Wars' Before. Will This Be It?|date=2018-05-30|work=[[Wikipedia:The New York Times|''The New York Times'']]|archivedate=20180530223747}} Another suggestion from Kasdan was to give Anakin Skywalker a little grey beard to give him a little normality upon being unmasked. In regards to Luke's confrontation with the Emperor, Kasdan suggested Lucas to have Luke pretending to join the Emperor by putting on [[Darth]] Vader's [[Darth Vader's armor|mask]] and going to the second Death Star's controls but fire on Had Abbadon, the [[Galactic Empire|Imperial]] homeworld, instead of destroying the [[Alliance to Restore the Republic|Rebel Alliance's]] [[fleet]], but this twist didn't make it when Had Abaddon was dropped from the film. Prior to Had Abaddon's abandonement, Kasdan constantly argued with Lucas during pre-production on whether if it was better to scrap [[Endor]]'s presence for the [[Battle of Endor|climatic battle]] and settle it on the Imperial capital. In September 1981, Kasdan delivered his second draft for ''Return of the Jedi'', but revisions continued on it until November and a third draft was written in December.''[[From Star Wars to Jedi: The Making of a Saga]]'' In the end, to satisfy the fans emotionally, Kasdan felt that ''Jedi'' should be a combination of the first two ''Star Wars'' films, including the fun of the original film and the positive values given in the second film. In respect of the film's title, Kasdan told Lucas that he opined that ''Return of the Jedi'' was a weak title, leading Lucas to change it to ''Revenge of the Jedi''. However, Lucas ultimately felt that the title was wrong because the Jedi don't seek revenge and opted to rename the film back to ''Return of the Jedi'', though some teaser posters with the "Revenge" word drawn by [[Drew Struzan]] in the title had already been distributed.''[[The Star Wars Poster Book]]'' ''Return of the Jedi'' was released on [[May 25]], [[1983]]. Even before the film's release, Kasdan stated that he would not write any other ''Star Wars'' film, being correctly sure that Lucas would start shooting the prequel trilogy next, but also stated that he would never say never, as he didn't think that he would even write ''Jedi'' in the first place. ===''The Force Awakens''=== {{Quote|There were many times over the ensuing years when George [asked] me to be involved in all three [prequels]. He said, 'Hey, how would you like to write such-and-such?' I said, 'George, aren't you supposed to start shooting in two weeks, in Australia?' He said, 'Yeah, but it's not too late.'|Lawrence Kasdan on George Lucas|}} In the following years after the release of ''Return of the Jedi'', Lucas developed the ''Star Wars'' prequel trilogy, consisting of [[Star Wars: Episode I The Phantom Menace|''The Phantom Menace'']], [[Star Wars: Episode II Attack of the Clones|''Attack of the Clones'']] and [[Star Wars: Episode III Revenge of the Sith|''Revenge of the Sith'']], released in [[1999]], [[2002]], and [[2005]] respectively. During the development of the prequels, Lucas constantly approached Kasdan to them, even during the first screening of ''The Phantom Menace'', but Kasdan resisted all offers to return to the franchise though Lucas thought that it would be great for Kasdan to take a second pass on the script. Kasdan opined that after some of the creative clashes against him, Kershner, and Marquand during the original trilogy, Lucas should do the prequels just as he wanted.''Eon Magazine'', September 1999 As revealed in late [[2022]], Kasdan had practically no relationship with the prequel films.{{Lucasfilm|url=news/jon-kasdan-willow-interview|text=Jon Kasdan's ''Willow'' Adventure|date=2022-11-30|archivedate=20221204144116}} Incidentally, one of the most criticized aspects of the prequel trilogy was its dialogue, which critics feeling it "too corny and cheesy," despite Lucas' opinion that the dialogue was tongue-in-cheek because of the prequels' underlying motif as a melodrama and nature as a soap opera.''[[The Star Wars Archives: Episodes I–III, 1999–2005]]'' In early [[2012]], Kasdan was contacted by [[Kathleen Kennedy]] to talk with her and Lucas, as the latter was planning to retire and sell [[Lucasfilm]] to [[The Walt Disney Company]], but part of the deal involved the production of more ''Star Wars'' movies. While Kasdan had doubts on whether to accept the offer, he gladly accepted it when Kennedy and Lucas offered him to make a film centered on Han Solo.{{WebCite|url=https://www.slashfilm.com/lawrence-and-jonathan-kasdan-interview-solo|text=Lawrence and Jonathan Kasdan on Writing Han Solo's Origin and Figuring Out the Kessel Run [Interview]|author=Sciretta, Peter|date=2018-05-25|archivedate=20181203173008}} Kennedy and Lucas also wanted Kasdan to consult the script for [[Star Wars: Episode VII The Force Awakens|''Star Wars'': Episode VII]], which Kasdan offered to do once a month before settling on consulting the seventh installment of the saga and writing the Han Solo movie.{{SW|url=news/solo-lawrence-and-jonathan-kasdan-interview|text=A Portrait Of The Scoundrel As A Young Man: Writers Lawrence And Jonathan Kasdan On ''Solo: A Star Wars Story''}} On [[October 30]], Lucasfilm announced its acquisition by Disney and that among the new films, there would be a ''Star Wars'' [[sequel trilogy]] which would continue the saga after ''Return of the Jedi''.{{SW|url=news/new-star-wars-movies-announced-as-disney-enters-agreement-to-acquire-lucasfilm-ltd|text=New ''Star Wars'' Movies Announced as Disney Enters Agreement to Acquire Lucasfilm Ltd.}} Reports surfaced on [[November 20]] that Kasdan, along with screenwriter [[Simon Kinberg]], would be co-producing all three films of the upcoming sequel trilogy, and would share writing responsibilities with Kinberg on the [[Star Wars: Episode VIII The Last Jedi|eight]] and [[Star Wars: Episode IX The Rise of Skywalker|ninth films]].{{WebCite|url=https://www.hollywoodreporter.com/heat-vision/star-wars-lawrence-kasdan-simon-393459|text='Star Wars': Lawrence Kasdan, Simon Kinberg to Write, Produce - Hollywood Reporter|author=Kit, Borys|date=2012-11-20|archivedate=20200803104807}} Subsequent reports, citing insider confirmation, suggested that Kasdan and Kinberg would indeed be writing ''Star Wars'' projects, just not necessarily ''Episodes VIII'' and ''IX''.{{WebCite|url=https://www.hollywoodreporter.com/news/post-george-lucas-star-wars-394910|text=Post-George Lucas 'Star Wars': How Writers Lawrence Kasdan and Simon Kinberg Will Expand the Galaxy - Hollywood Reporter|author=Kit, Borys|date=2012-11-28|work=[[Wikipedia:The Hollywood Reporter|''The Hollywood Reporter'']]|archivedate=20200521053642}} However, [[StarWars.com]] confirmed on [[January 25]], [[2013]] that Kasdan and [[J.J. Abrams]] would serve as the screenwriters for Episode VII, with Abrams also directing the film.{{SW|url=news/master-filmmaking-team-announced-for-star-wars|text=Master Filmmaking Team Announced for ''Star Wars'': Episode VII}} Kasdan was excited upon learning of Abrams' hiring, because he thought that Abrams was the right person to direct the film, finding him funny and smart once they met.{{WebCite|url=https://www.latimes.com/entertainment/herocomplex/la-ca-hc-star-wars-lawrence-kasdan-20151206-story.html|text=Q&A: Star Wars screenwriter Lawrence Kasdan on the past, present and future of 'Star Wars'|author=Rottenberg, Josh|date=2015-12-03|work=[[Wikipedia:Los Angeles Times|''Los Angeles Times'']]|archivedate=20151204033853}} On [[February 5]], Disney confirmed that Kasdan was also working along with Kinberg on new standalone films, not Episodes VIII and IX as reported two and a half months earlier.{{WebCite|url=http://www.slashfilm.com/disney-confirms-standalone-star-wars-films-may-be-released-between-new-core-episodes|text=Disney Confirms Standalone Star Wars Films; May Be Released Between New Core Episodes|author=Fischer, Russ|date=2013-02-05|archivedate=20180103083211}} Feeling the original trilogy to be part of his "DNA," Kasdan didn't rewatch the first three films of the franchise, as his and Abrams' intent was to bring something new and different to the saga with Abrams as director, much like how Irvin Kershner and Richard Marquand imposed their respective visions in ''The Empire Strikes Back'' and ''Return of the Jedi''. [[File:Episode_7_Cast.jpg|thumb|300px|Kasdan along with Abrams and the cast of ''Star Wars: The Force Awakens'']] [[Michael Arndt]] had been initially hired to write Episode VII, so Kasdan was brought into the project to see if he could consult on Arndt's script. Kasdan also met with Episode VIII director [[Rian Johnson]] to have a week-long story session.''[[The Art of Star Wars: The Last Jedi]]'' Throughout 2013, Kasdan had many meetings with Ardnt and the planning group, including Kennedy and [[Kiri Hart]] from the [[Lucasfilm Story Group]]. He reunited with Arndt and the filmmakers at a hotel in [[Wikipedia:Santa Monica, California|Santa Monica]] to figure out the story for Episode VII, but they struggled to come up with a story for months and production was approaching, leading Arndt to leave the film and hand the screenwriting duties to Kasdan and Abrams. Writing the script, Kasdan felt that it was Han Solo the character the audience finds irresistible instead of Luke, comparing Solo to [[Wikipedia:William Holden|William Holden]], [[Wikipedia:Jimmy Cagney|Jimmy Cagney]] and [[Wikipedia:Humphrey Bogart|Humphrey Bogart]] due to being forced to be reluctantly heroic and altruistic.''The Director Within: Storytellers of Stage and Screen'' Kasdan had always feel that Han impressed the audience for his unpredictability and willingness to risk everything. He found irresistible to write to revisit the character of Han Solo, now older but no wiser. Acknowleding that thirty years had passed in-universe since they had last seen the iconic [[smuggler]], Kasdan opined that Han should commit the same mistakes again and again because while he was no longer young, his personality remained the same, but he had also been changed by some experiences, regrets and disappointments, which Kasdan knew were the basis of the character. Writing the original trilogy trio thirty years after their last appearance was fun for Kasdan, as he had aged just like Ford, [[Carrie Fisher]] and [[Mark Hamill]]. The film finally killed off Han Solo just like Kasdan and Ford had originally desired since ''Return of the Jedi'', though Kasdan felt that Ford may have rethought about killing Solo during the interim between the original and sequel films. Abrams initially feared taking the decision to kill off Solo, but Kasdan convinced him to go ahead with it, being enthusiastic about the idea to Abrams' relief, who felt that Kasdan made him feel that the idea wasn't necessarily the worst one.{{WebCite|url=https://ew.com/article/2015/12/21/jj-abrams-kylo-ren-shocking-act-star-wars-force-awakens|text=We Need To Talk About Kylo|author=Breznican, Anthony|date=2015-12-21|work=''[[Entertainment Weekly]]''|archivedate=20200613191901}} As for Luke Skywalker's presence in the film, Arndt's script introduced Luke Skywalker halfway through the film, but as Luke's appearance upstaged the protagonist, Kasdan and Abrams ultimately agreed with Arndt prior to his departure that Luke should be withdrawed from the story and become the film's [[Wikipedia:MacGuffin|MacGuffin]]. Similarly, [[R2-D2]] and [[C-3PO]] were initially written to appear together as in previous films, but Kasdan intelligently suggested Arndt to sideline Artoo as well and keep him separate from Threepio, so the former could have his own "awakening" at the end. Artoo could also serve as a plot device just like Luke, being part of the [[Map to Skywalker|missing framework]] to find the legendary Jedi, with Kasdan, Arndt and Abrams thinking that he could have downloaded vital information when he plugged into the [[DS-1 Death Star Mobile Battle Station|Death Star]] back in the original film.{{WebCite|url=https://ew.com/article/2015/12/20/jj-abrams-answers-burning-question-about-r2-d2-star-wars-force-awakens|text=Star Wars The Force Awakens: J.J. Abrams explains R2-D2's closing scene|author=Breznican, Anthony|date=2015-12-20|archivedate=20190315134713}} Purposely, Kasdan and Abrams looked at what had come before, telling a new story that touched specific themes and ideas that had been previously seen. They didn't immediately get to use ideas like bringing Emperor Palpatine back and relating him to the protagonist, but acknowledged these could be revised later on.{{WebCite|url=https://uproxx.com/movies/jj-abrams-interview-the-rise-of-skywalker-star-wars|text=J.J. Abrams On 'The Rise Of Skywalker' And The Return Of Palpatine (And, Yes, Maclunkey)|author=Ryan, Mike|date=2019-11-25|archivedate=20200113233521}} Han Solo and the original trilogy characters aside, Kasdan and Abrams also had to develop new characters to carry on the sequel trilogy. The whole movie consisted in a series of character introductions, whether new or familiar. From the initial discussions with Abrams, Kasdan became aware of Abrams' excitement at the concept of having a woman at the centre of the trilogy's story, which led to the creation of [[Rey Skywalker|Rey]].{{WebCite|url=https://www.theguardian.com/film/2015/dec/17/star-wars-director-jj-abrams-women-the-force-awakens-daisy-ridley-lupita-nyongo-carrie-fisher|text=Star Wars director JJ Abrams: we always wanted women at the centre of The Force Awakens|author=Pulver, Andrew|date=2015-12-11|work=[[Wikipedia:The Guardian|''The Guardian'']]|archivedate=20151217202202}} The character of [[Ben Solo|Kylo Ren]], the main antagonist, was unique for Kasdan and brand new to the saga, describing him as full of emotion, which he deals with the best he can, being ultimately impressed with how [[Adam Driver]] ended up playing the part in the finished film.{{WebCite|url=https://ew.com/article/2015/08/12/star-wars-force-awakens-kylo-ren|text=Star Wars: The Force Awakens: Kylo Ren's shroud is pulled back at last|author=Breznican, Anthony|date=2015-08-15|work=''[[Entertainment Weekly]]''|archivedate=20150813235359}} Kasdan also conceived the idea of making the character [[Finn]] a deserting [[First Order stormtrooper|stormtrooper]], feeling that it would be incredible to have one of their protagonists be a member of a known group of "faceless, indistinguishable automatons."Secrets of ''The Force Awakens'': A Cinematic Journey (Chapter 1) Kasdan first had the idea before Arndt departed the production, as Arndt recalled how exasperated Kasdan blurted out such backstory as they struggled to find an original background for their male lead.{{WebCite|url=https://ew.com/article/2016/01/16/ew-podcast-star-wars-force-awakens-creators-discuss-secrets-universe|text=EW Podcast: ''Star Wars: The Force Awakens''|author=Anthony Breznican|date=2016-01-16|work=''[[Entertainment Weekly]]''|archivedate=20200730091528}} Kasdan and Abrams wrote the screenplay for Episode VII, which was titled ''The Force Awakens'', under time pressure, but Kasdan found the writing process as the most funniest from his career. In addition to Santa Monica, Kasdan and Abrams discussed about the story in [[Wikipedia:New York City|New York City]], [[Wikipedia:Paris|Paris]], [[Wikipedia:London|London]]; discussions which Abrams recorded on his [[Wikipedia:iPhone|iPhone]]. They even worked energized right through Christmas 2013. Their first draft was completed in six weeks. The changes of Kasdan and Abrams to Arndt's initial script stalled the casting process of ''The Force Awakens'' for a while, but it finally began in earnest in January [[2014]],{{WebCite|url=https://ew.com/article/2014/05/15/star-wars-episode-vii-new-hope-really|text='Star Wars Episode VII': A New Hope (Really)|author=EW Staff|date=2014-05-15|work=''[[Entertainment Weekly]]''|archivedate=20210202061200}} which was when Kasdan and Abrams delivered their first draft, relieving the production crew, who now had so much to prep and build in [[Wikipedia:England|England]]. Since January to the start of production, Kasdan and Abrams continued to write some parts of the script even during filming. ''The Force Awakens'' was on [[December 18]], [[2015]]. Kasdan's return to the franchise for the film was especially praised by [[Wikipedia:The Hollywood Reporter|''The Hollywood Reporter'']]{{'s}} reviewer [[Wikipedia:Todd McCarthy|Todd McCarthy]], who felt that ''Awakens'' let feel Kasdan's hand into the story, which McCarthy compared to ''Raiders of the Lost Ark'' in terms of its exuberance and incident.{{WebCite|url=https://www.hollywoodreporter.com/review/star-wars-force-awakens-review-848963|text='Star Wars: The Force Awakens': Film Review|author=McCarthy, Todd|date=2015-12-18|work=[[Wikipedia:The Hollywood Reporter|''The Hollywood Reporter'']]|archivedate=20151216215525}} With his work finished in ''The Force Awakens'', Kasdan and Abrams left the trilogy in the hands of Rian Johnson and [[Colin Trevorrow]]; Kasdan had faith that Johnson, as a friend of his, would make an unexpected and weirder sequel. However, Trevorrow was ultimately replaced with Abrams, so Kasdan later met with him and his creative team alongside Lucas and Johnson to develop ideas for the script of Episode IX, which was titled ''The Rise of Skywalker''.{{WebCite|url=https://www.starwarsnewsnet.com/2019/04/rise-of-skywalker-j-j-abrams-consulted.html|text=Star Wars: The Rise Of Skywalker – J. J. Abrams Consulted With George Lucas, Lawrence Kasdan, and Rian Johnson|author=Davis, Grant|date=2019-04-12|archivedate=20200127141956}} ===''Solo''=== After working on ''The Force Awakens'', Kasdan turned his attention back on the Han Solo-centric film Lucasfilm had initially asked him to write. As part of the [[Star Wars Anthology Series|''Star Wars'' Anthology]] line, an intended stand-alone film series focused on the origin stories of some ''Star Wars'' characters, the film was intended to tell Han's origin story. Though reluctant to return at first, Kasdan decided to do so as Solo, without doubt, was his favorite character. While talking with Michael Arndt and Simon Kinberg, Kasdan explained to them his own idea of how Han got his last name, assuring that he had no family to [[Drawd Munbrin|a person]] who ended up giving him the surname "Solo." Kasdan pitched his idea by telling Disney CEO [[Bob Iger (chief executive officer)|Bob Iger]] about this scene, which impressed Iger and led him to greenlight the movie.{{WebCite|url=https://www.syfy.com/syfywire/the-most-important-line-in-solo-a-star-wars-story-was-the-first-one-written|text=The most important line in Solo: A Star Wars Story was the first one written|author=Zakarin, Jordan|date=2018-05-15|archivedate=20190525031722}} Kasdan wrote the script between the story meetings for ''The Force Awakens'', but stopped working on it once he was asked by Kathleen Kennedy to help Abrams with Episode VII's script. He was left with almost no time to write the Han movie given his and Abrams' constant rewrites during Episode VII's production. As he worked in ''The Force Awakens'' As he worked in ''The Force Awakens'', Kasdan left his son [[Jon Kasdan|Jon]] to write the Han Solo film until he returned. Once his screenwriting duties for ''The Force Awakens'' finished, Kasdan was unsure if he wanted to still write the film, but Lucasfilm insisted their interest to still make it, which led Kasdan to ask them if they could hire Jon too to work with him because of his enthusiasm and creativity.{{WebCite|url=https://www.digitalspy.com/movies/a849876/george-lucas-solo-a-star-wars-story-han-solo-movie-disney-lucasfilm|text=George Lucas was already developing a Han Solo movie before Disney bought Lucasfilm|author=McCreesh, Louise|date=2018-02-13|work=[[Wikipedia:Digital Spy|''Digital Spy'']]|archivedate=20200414003513}} Kasdan credited his son for reawakening his interest in the project, reminding him everything why had gotten him excited when he started working on the script, his love for the Han Solo character and that they could make a different ''Star Wars'' movie, touching many of the film genres they liked like [[Wikipedia:Film noir|film noir]] or [[Wikipedia:Western (genre)|Westerns]]. Since he was a kid, along with his brother [[Wikipedia:Jake Kasdan|Jake]], Jon had tried to pressure his father into returning to the franchise. Kasdan even brought Jon with him to visit the set of ''The Force Awakens'', even allowing Jon to be involved in some pivotal scenes like Han's death. Though Jon had followed his father's steps by becoming a filmmaker, father and son had never written a movie together. Lucasfilm approved Kasdan's idea and negotiated with Jon to make the film. Thus, father and son started working on the script in 2015. Like his father, Jon was also a fan of the Han Solo character. One of the aspects the Kasdans appreciated mostly from Solo was his recklessness in his relationships and life, inspiring them to write how Solo got to form that attitude toward life. To direct the film, Kasdan insisted in hiring the filmmaker duo of [[Phil Lord]] and [[Christopher Miller]], feeling they were the right directors to fill Han's mysterious backstory.{{WebCite|url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/films/2016/07/17/star-wars-episode-viii-will-be-classic-war-film-like-the-bridge|text=Star Wars: Episode VIII will be classic war film like The Bridge on the River Kwai, director reveals|author=Collin, Robbie|date=2016-07-17|work=[https://www.telegraph.co.uk The Telegraph]|archivedate=20190523144859}} On [[July 7]], 2015, Lord and Miller were confirmed to direct the film, with the Kasdans expressing delight on working with the duo to bring a fresh, new dimension to the franchise.{{SW|url=news/christopher-miller-and-phil-lord-to-helm-han-solo-anthology-film|text=Christopher Miller and Phil Lord to Helm Han Solo Anthology Film}} [[File:JonKasdan.jpg|thumb|left|200px|Jon Kasdan, Lawrence's son, who helped him to write ''Solo'']] The Kasdans worked on the script for nine months. Kasdan found the tone of the script, that of a crime Western, different in comparison to the main ''Star Wars'' films, where there were always the Rebels vs. Empire dynamic. They also added more explicit romance in the film compared to Han's brief flirting in the original trilogy.{{WebCite|url=https://www.gamesradar.com/the-writers-of-solo-a-star-wars-story-discuss-the-films-tone-were-not-dealing-with-the-spiritual-side|text=The Solo: A Star Wars Story writers discuss the film's tone: "We're not dealing with the spiritual side"|author=Prell, Sam|date=2018-04-28|work=[[Wikipedia:SFX (magazine)|''SFX'']] #300|archiveurl=https://archive.is/nsgWb}} Kasdan didn't see the film as an origin story like Jon brilliantly put it up, but as a rite of passage, focused on human concerns instead of [[the Force]], the Jedi and religion; how Han was formed and why did he end up doing the things he later did during the saga. Kasdan also saw the film as a love story about Han and [[Chewbacca]], whom Kasdan saw as a great and fun foil to Han, because their friendship is the best one from the saga, which Jon compared to one with [[Wikipedia:Spencer Tracy|Spencer Tracy]] and [[Wikipedia:Katherine Hepburn|Katherine Hepburn]], which they found challenging to write. For the story, the Kasdans took inspiration from some Western and crime movies like [[Wikipedia:Heat (1995 film)|''Heat'']], [[Wikipedia:Unforgiven|''Unforgiven'']], [[Wikipedia:Gangster No. 1|''Gangster No. 1'']] and various movies of the [[Wikipedia:Coen brothers|Coen brothers]] like [[Wikipedia:The Big Lebowski|''The Big Lebowski'']] as well from [[Wikipedia:Robert Louis Stevenson|Robert Louis Stevenson's]] classic novel [[Wikipedia:Treasure Island|''Treasure Island'']]; the latter being also an adventure story of a young boy who meets characters of dubious intent,{{WebCite|url=https://ew.com/movies/2018/02/12/solo-a-star-wars-story-influences|text=How ''Treasure Island'', ''The Big Lebowski'', and ''Heat'' inspired ''Solo: A Star Wars Story''|author=Breznican, Anthony|date=2018-02-12|work=''[[Entertainment Weekly]]''|archivedate=20200316213644}} which the Kasdans read again before diving into the script.{{WebCite|url=https://ew.com/movies/2018/02/09/solo-a-star-wars-story-woody-harrelson-thandie-newton-paul-bettany|text=''Star Wars'': Woody Harrelson, Thandie Newton, Paul Bettany character details|author=Breznican, Anthony|date=2018-02-09|work=''[[Entertainment Weekly]]''|archivedate=20200527050119}} Before Jon or anyone else joined the project, Kasdan had conceived the notion of a [[Wikipedia:Charles Dickens|Charles Dickens]]-esque childhood for Han as well as the character of [[Qi'ra]], Han's romantic interest whom Kasdan named "Kura" at first.{{Twitter|jonkasdan|status/1040529501870944256|[[Jon Kasdan]]|quote=In honor of the digital release of #SoloAStarWarsStory & in lieu of a commentary, here are 53 random factoids & notes about the making & writing for anybody who's interested. If you haven't seen the movie yet… maybe watch it before reading? Anyway, enjoy the movie & #MTFBWY|archivedate=20200511115830}} It was also decided from the beginning of the script's development that Qi'ra would end up betraying and leaving Han, as she was intended to be more complicated and ambitious than Han himself at that point in their lives.{{Twitter|JonKasdan|status/1040529511513616385|[[Jon Kasdan]]|quote=continued|archivedate=20181002054217}} An aspect from the script present in every draft was the [[20-T Railcrawler conveyex transport|Conveyex transport's]] [[Heist on Vandor|job]], though the cargo was originally intended to be an extrmeely-dangerous criminal instead of [[coaxium]]. To invoke the sense of a space Western, the Kasdans chose to create [[Vandor-1]] to evoke the beautiful landscape from the [[Wikipedia:Southern Rocky Mountains|Southern Rocky Mountains]] of [[Wikipedia:Colorado|Colorado]], where the Kasdans spend many Christmas together. Another sequence stuck from the very first draft was that of the [[Akkadese Maelstrom]], which the Kasdans discussed endlessly with the crew because they wanted it as a means to make the [[Kessel Run]] visually exciting, wanting to give a [[Wikipedia:Jules Verne|Jules Verne]] flavor to the scene.{{Twitter|JonKasdan|status/1040529505670946817|[[Jon Kasdan]]|quote=continued|archivefile=File:Kasdan_Solo_Trivia_2.png|archivedate=20200114055629}} Among some of the things Lord and Miller added to the script were the inclusion of the speeder chase at the beginning. Kasdan was instead more passionate about having a foot-chase to evoke the Dickesian element and an scene in which Han pulls out an eel from his pants as a nod to [[Wikipedia:River Phoenix|River Phoenix]], who previously worked with Kasdan in his film [[Wikipedia:I Love You to Death|''I Love You to Death'']], though the scene was ultimately [[Scenes cut from Star Wars|cut]] from the finished film. When it was decided to also cut the scene where Han is [[Tribunal of Han Solo|expelled]] for disobeying direct orders at the [[Academy of Carida|Imperial Academy]], the Kasdans fought hard to keep the scene in the film, as they saw it crucial to show Han as a pilot, but it was ultimately deemed to be a hinderance to the story's flow, which consistently felt to start upon Chewbacca's arrival. The Kasdans also intended to include a spooky [[Wikipedia:Ridley Scott|Ridley Scott]]-type planet full of [[Wikipedia:Lovecraftian horror|Lovecraftian]] monsters, where the protagonists would be forced to pit stop, but Lord and Miller correctly determined that the pit-stop would interrupt the Kessel Run's momentum. The film's characters represented facets of Han's formation and were inspired by one of the most common themes in ''Star Wars'', which consists that anyone can find a new family if his original one may be gone, changing each other by their contact or meeting as strangers at first, like is the case of the first meetings of Chewbacca and [[Landonis Balthazar Calrissian|Lando Calrissian]] with Han, or even forgetting about old acquaintances, like it happens with Han's relationship with Qi'ra, which changes over the movie's course. An early inspiration Kasdan had for the character if Qi'ra was that of [[Wikipedia:Jane Greer|Jane Greer's]] Kathie Moffat from the seminal film noir film [[Wikipedia:Out of the Past|''Out of the Past'']].''[[The Art of Solo: A Star Wars Story]]'' As a movie styled with film noir, its absolute center had to be a [[Wikipedia:Femme fatale|femme fatale]] with a dark duality about whether to give her love to the people she cares or let her selfishness get her best, a role which the Kasdans found perfect for Qi'ra, approving [[Emilia Clarke]]'s casting as her, whom Kasdan thought to be one of the best ''Star Wars'' women, being a fan of Clarke's work in [[Wikipedia:Game of Thrones|''Game of Thrones'']]. The Kasdans based [[Tobias Beckett]] on [[Wikipedia:Long John Silver|Long John Silver]], the main antagonist of ''Treasure Island'', whose mentor/apprentice relationship with Jim Hawkins they wanted to recreate between Beckett and Solo. The Kasdans approved [[Woody Harrelson]]'s casting as Beckett, feeling that he could embody the duality of appearing as a figure who can be seen as a teacher, a father figure, a rascal, an unreliable narrator or an unrealiable partner. The character of [[L3-37]] was conceived in conversations between the Kasdans, Lord and Miller, coming from the latter's astute observation that it was funny how [[Wuher]] kicked out C-3PO in the first ''Star Wars'' film, given that droids seems to be the least rambunctious folks in [[the galaxy]]. With L3, the Kasdans saw an opportunity to add a new female character with a voice that was different from the others the audience had already seen. While Jon wrote Lando Calrissian as pansexual and romantically interested in L3-37, Kasdan was more cryptic about the intention of L3's jokes focusing on Lando's flirty attitude toward Han, musing that it may mean something or not, dismissing the dialogue as part of L3's personality.{{WebCite|url=https://www.huffpost.com/entry/lando-calrissian-sexual-fluidity-solo-star-wars_n_5af77d59e4b00d7e4c1b37a9|text='Star Wars' Writer Confirms Donald Glover's Character Is Pansexual In 'Solo'|author=Bradley, Bill|date=2018-05-17|work=[[Wikipedia:HuffPost|''HuffPost'']]|archivedate=20190503011331}} Early drafts by the Kasdans had [[Enfys Nest]] and her marauders attack Beckett's gang during the Vandor heist with [[Bantha-II cargo skiff]]s, but Lord and Miller preferred to use [[swoop]] bikes, so Jon browsed for inspirations at [[Wookieepedia]] until he found out the [[Cloud-Riders/Legends|Cloud-Riders]] swoop-biking gang from the classic [[Marvel Comics]]' [[Star Wars (1977)|''Star Wars'' series]] from the [[1970]]s. Jon told his father about his findings and they decided to include the [[Cloud-Riders]].{{Twitter|jonkasdan|status/995504590337466368|[[Jon Kasdan]]|quote=Not a coincidence at all. In the earliest drafts of the script Enfys Marauders attacked the Conveyex on Bantha II skiffs. When Chris & Phil decided they wanted to do swoop bikes instead, we found the Cloud Riders on Wookieepedia, from above Marvel comic, and it STUCK.|archivefile=File:Jon Kasdan used Wookieepedia.png}} An idea Lawrence was insisted by his son to include was bringing [[Warwick Davis]] back as his ''The Phantom Menace'' character [[Weazel]] as part of the Cloud-Riders. Due to not seeing the prequels, Lawrence had no idea of who Weazel was, but trusted his son to write the character in the film. Respect to some sequences, one that survived several drafts fairly deep into production before being dropped involved [[Beckett's gang]] leaving [[Mimban]] without Solo and Chewbacca, leading the duo to steal an Imperial garbage ship to escape, dumping the garbage onto several [[stormtrooper]]s in the process. To figure out how to write the [[Raid on Kessel|Kessel Heist]] to have the kind of feeling and momentum of coordinated effort, the Kasdans kept revisiting the [[Wikipedia:Mission: impossible (film series)|''Mission: Impossible'' film series]], though Han's personality constrasts that of [[Wikipedia:Ethan Hunt|Ethan Hunt]]. Once the script was finished, casting for the young Han Solo began, and [[Alden Ehrenreich]] was ultimately cast in the lead role.{{SW|url=news/alden-ehrenreich-is-han-solo|text=Aldren Ehrenreich Is Han Solo}} With Ehrenreich's casting, certain parts of the script were revised so they could be tailored to Ehrenreich's voice. Kathleen Kennedy also sent Harrison Ford a copy of the Kasdans' script for his approval.{{WebCite|url=https://ew.com/movies/2018/02/07/harrison-ford-solo-star-wars-story|text=Harrison Ford was a covert adviser to ''Solo: A Star Wars Story''|author=Breznican, Anthony|date=2018-02-07|work=[[Wikipedia:Entertainment Weekly|Entertainment Weekly]]|archivedate=20200609110114}} Filming began in [[2017]], but as filming went ahead, Lucasfilm felt that Lord and Miller were moving the movie away from the vision crafted by the Kasdans' script and significantly changing the story by encouraging an improvisational style on set.{{WebCite|url=https://ew.com/movies/2017/06/22/ron-howard-takes-over-directing-duties-on-han-solo-film|text=How the Han Solo film broke apart — with Ron Howard picking up the pieces|author=Breznican, Anthony|date=2017-06-22|work=''[[Entertainment Weekly]]''|archivedate=20191104071844}} To appease Kasdan, who was against improvisation, Lord and Miller shot alternate takes with the actors reciting the dialogue "word by word" and additional ones with improvised dialogue. As the measures didn't get the production on track, Kennedy requested Kasdan to come to [[Wikipedia:London|London]], but Lord and Miller felt that Kasdan had become something like a "shadow director."{{WebCite|url=https://www.hollywoodreporter.com/heat-vision/star-wars-han-solo-movie-firing-new-details-behind-phil-lord-chris-miller-exit-1016619|text='Star Wars' Firing Reveals a Disturbance in the Franchise|author=Masters, Kim|date=2017-06-26|work=[[Wikipedia:The Hollywood Reporter|''The Hollywood Reporter'']]|archivedate=20170626150930}} Though Lucasfilm believed they could fix all these issues with the reshoots, Lord and Miller were reportedly reluctant to alter their approach, which led Kennedy to remove them from the project. Kasdan felt that given their successes in comedies like [[Wikipedia:21 Jump Street (film)|''21 Jump Street'']] and [[Wikipedia:The LEGO Movie|''The LEGO Movie'']], Lord and Miller wanted to make fun of the film's tone and push it into the "[[Wikipedia:Guardians of the Galaxy (film)|''Guardians of the Galaxy'']] territory," which he refused, as he felt that though one can have fun with the tone, they should not make fun of the tone, having the sense that in nowadays' meta culture there is a tendency to make fun of films even before they are made. Having come up with the premise years ago, Kasdan felt some kind of ownership of the film, which led him to raise his voice to mantain and hear the franchise's tone.{{WebCite|url=https://abcnews.go.com/Entertainment/wireStory/solo-battle-soul-tone-star-wars-55239463|text=In 'Solo,' a battle for the soul, and tone, of 'Star Wars'|author=Coyle, Jake|date=2018-05-17|work=[https://abcnews.go.co abcnews.go.com]|archivedate=20180517213900}} Said and done, it was announced on [[June 20]], 2017 by Lucasfilm that a new director for the film would soon be announced as Lord and Miller were departing from the project due to creative differences.{{SW|url=news/a-message-from-lucasfilm-regarding-the-untitled-han-solo-film|text=A Message From Lucasfilm Regarding the Untitled Han Solo Film}} Once Lord and Miller left the project, Kennedy searched for a new director immediately, trying to have Kasdan assume the directorial duties of the film. However, Kasdan was prevented from doing so by the rules of the [[Wikipedia:Directors Guild of America|Directors Guild of America]], which established that someone already involved in a production which has lost a director can't serve as a replacement, leading Kennedy to consider [[Ron Howard]] and [[Joe Johnston]] as other potential options.{{WebCite|url=https://www.hollywoodreporter.com/heat-vision/star-wars-why-han-solo-movie-directors-were-fired-1015474|text='Star Wars': Why the Han Solo Film Directors Were Fired|author=Kit, Borys|date=2017-06-20|work=[[Wikipedia:The Hollywood Reporter|''The Hollywood Reporter'']]|archivedate=20170621051140}} Howard ultimately agreed to replace Lord and Miller and joined the project [[June 22|two days later]].{{SW|url=news/ron-howard-to-assume-directorial-duties-on-the-untitled-han-solo-film|text=Ron Howard to Assume Directorial Duties on the Untitled Han Solo Film}} Kasdan approved Howard's hiring, as he invited him and Jon to talk and expressed his love towards their script. Howard then directed some reshoots, during which Howard brought back the subject of including the Lovecraftian monsters the Kasdans had initially written, leading to the creation of the [[Summa-verminoth]]. Filming finished on [[October 17]] and its title was officially announced to be ''[[Solo: A Star Wars Story]]''.{{WebCite|url=https://ew.com/movies/2017/10/17/the-han-solo-movie-has-a-title-solo|text=The Han Solo movie has a title — Solo|author=Breznican, Anthony|date=2017-10-17|work=''[[Entertainment Weekly]]''|archivedate=20190619220925}} ''Solo: A Star Wars Story'' was released on [[May 25]], [[2018]]. The Kasdans were praised by [[Wikipedia:Forbes|''Forbes'']] incidentally for making the film feel like a random adventure rather than the usual "Empire versus Rebellion" struggle,{{WebCite|url=https://www.forbes.com/sites/scottmendelson/2018/05/15/solo-review-a-star-wars-story-that-plays-like-an-indiana-jones-movie|text='Solo' Review: A 'Star Wars' Story That Plays Like An 'Indiana Jones' Movie|author=Mendelson, Scott|date=2018-05-15|work=[[Wikipedia:Forbes|''Forbes'']]|archivedate=20180713011935}} while [[Wikipedia:The Guardian|''The Guardian'']] credited them for "channeling the spirits" of the original trilogy into the film.{{WebCite|url=https://www.theguardian.com/film/2018/may/15/solo-a-star-wars-story-review-han-solo-origins-film-is-boistrous-bromance|text=Solo: A Star Wars Story review – Han Solo origins film is boisterous bromance|author=Bradshaw, Peter|date=2018-05-15|work=[[Wikipedia:The Guardian|The Guardian]]|archivedate=20200620021054}} However, ''Solo'' ended up underperforming, something which Kasdan felt unusual. Kasdan later stated that he felt that Lucasfilm "blew it" in regards to ''Solo''. Furthermore, following the film's flop, the studio repeatedly tried to woo him back, even sending a messenger with a new ''Star Wars'' script, but Kasdan was not interested and didn't answer it, as he just wanted to get back to directing, which has always been his true passion.{{WebCite|url=https://www.syfy.com/syfywire/lawrence-kasdan-done-with-star-wars-austin-film-festival|text=Austin Film Fest: Lawrence Kasdan on sequels, Solo, and why he's done with Star Wars|author=Long, Christian|date=2019-10-27|work=syfy.com|archivedate=20200407181053}} ==Sources== {{ScrollBox| *''[[The Empire Strikes
